1: A hash map trades space for time — we store past elements to avoid the O(n²) brute-force comparison.
2: Use a carry variable to handle sums ≥ 10, continuing to the next most significant digit.
3: The sliding window ensures each character is visited at most twice — once by each pointer — achieving O(n) time.
4: By partitioning the smaller array, we implicitly partition the larger one — achieving O(log(min(m,n))) instead of O(m+n).
5: There are 2n−1 possible centers in a string of length n (including gaps between characters). Expanding from each center is O(n) per center.
6: Track direction (down/up) and flip when reaching row 0 or the last row — this gives an O(n) one-pass solution.
7: Check overflow before it happens: if rev > INT_MAX/10 or rev < INT_MIN/10, the next digit will cause overflow.
8: Process the string character by character with a state machine: whitespace → sign → digits → end — early exit on non-digit.
21: A dummy head eliminates edge-case handling for the initial empty result list, making the code cleaner and less error-prone.
28: Only need to search up to (len(haystack) − len(needle)) to avoid unnecessary comparisons.
36: A flat set with encoded keys like `(5) in row 1` lets us validate all three constraints in a single pass through the board.
42: At any position, water trapped = min(max left height, max right height) − current height. The two-pointer method computes this in one pass.
48: Transpose + reverse each row is a cleaner mental model than rotating four cells at a time — both are O(n²) but this is far less error-prone.
69: Binary search on a monotonic function (the square of a number is monotonically increasing for non-negative inputs).
70: Base cases: 1 way to reach stair 1 (1 step), 2 ways to reach stair 2 (1+1 or 2). Each subsequent stair is just the sum of the previous two.
102: A queue lets us process nodes in FIFO order, ensuring we visit children in left-to-right sequence within each level.
